i want to create a dataverse for my app driven by Bubble and I need your help. Its a logistics and business super app. We have users who can still be customers and service providers on the super app called dropy. So a shop owner can still switch accounts and order items from a shop within dropy and also a logistics provider can do the same. Our Dropy Super app has shops, parcels, rides, water, garbage, emergencies, logistics and more. For shops we have a unique idea, shop owners get to create shops, and add required details to make their shop usable. select which shop category they belong, add products and all product details including advanced product details like item color whenever necessary. We support different kinds of shop business models. Shops that sell items and those that offer services. For now only laundry and dispenser bottle refillers fall in that category. For shops that sell items shop owners create their own catalog of items with their respective desired menus for product assortment purposes. Those products have unique IDs for the purposes of having a QR tag which we need stored so that shop owners can share those qr codes of the products for purposes of marketing and also a new feature we have on our app called quick pay, where the product QR codes can be printed from the app then stuck on physical items to allow customers to scan and par or scan and add to cart and checkout if its multiple products from the same shop. The scan and pay is seamless because we have a wallet in which upon scanning the customer can enter a PIN to allow the money to be deducted from their wallet. We have a wallet for all the users but also the service providers have their respective wallets so that they don't get mixed up having business and personal money mixed up. For the shops, customers can search using a strong search engine to find items from our backend so that they can add to their carts. When a customer finds a desired product, they add on their cart and place the order to the shop first before the purchase. there is a place order button on the cart page that remains inactive after being clicked until the shop can process the order. Placing an order means the shop will see an incoming order from a customer and will then verify if the product is in stock not just online but also in the physical store since you realize shop owners are not just limited to selling using Dropy. Some shops have a physical presence where customers walk in and maybe they end up buying the products that are showing as available on the Dropy app but are not available on the physical store. This way the shop owner accepts the order by responding using a checklist to check the items the customer wanted. If the item is not available at the shop the shop owner clicks a button for out of stock, that way customer does not pay for something unavailable. When a product is in stock the shop owner clicks on process order and now the customer will see the inactive button that he clicked on to place an order turned to clickable and changes the text to checkout. If the shop owner does not have the product the customer will see the items as out of stock and the checkout button becomes unclickable since there's nothing to checkout. If a product has been checked out, the shop owner does not prepare the item until the payment has been confirmed. So the customer goes to the payment screen where he tops up money to his wallet using Visa or any other payment provider, Once money has been topped up, it can then be deducted and that money goes to an escrow account awaiting the order to be delivered for the money to be given to the shop owner and delivery guy. On the cart, there is still a place like a plus icon that is for adding a delivery note. customers can shop from multiple shops but we make sure that the money in their wallet for the specific transaction is held for that specific transaction so that we don't have a scenario where the customer orders items and does not have enough money in his wallet to facilitate the payment. For the wallet, there is a top-up, withdrawal, and transaction history. After the customer has paid successfully, the shop owner can then click on start after he enters the time he needs in minutes to prepare the order via an input field. When the shop owner is ready before the timer has finished the countdown they can still press the order ready button that now sends a notification to a nearby delivery guy to come pick up the order. Because a shop has multiple orders they are handling for multiple customers we have small rectangular cards for each order with a colored border that changes from green to red if the order has been delayed in terms of processing. that way the shop owner can adjust the timer and this notifies the customer as well so that the real-time tracking becomes effective. We have in-app notifications and also a screen where the customer can view the progress of their orders. Once the order has been fully processed by the shop owner, they press the order ready button which by default generates a QR code for that order when the delivery person arrives, they come and scan the QR to signify that they have picked up the order from the shop and now its en route to the customer. Once the rider arrives at the customer the customer now has to scan the QR from the rider's phone or we also have a 4-digit SMS code that we generate as well for the customer to complete the delivery and signify that they have collected the order from the rider or delivery guy. For the rider on their end, when they receive incoming orders, they can choose to accept or decline. When they click decline, that request is given to another rider in the vicinity or nearby. When the rider clicks accept, they are given a route on where they can go pick up the item from the shop, and after they have scanned the QR at the shop they see the line on the map that draws a path to the customers' destination. The first path was dotted to the shop then the one to the customer is a continuous bold line. He also gets the estimated time of arrival details and customer details and also the shop details on the screen so that he can either message using dropy messaging to the shop at that particular stage or call respectively. Sometimes the delivery person gets into a breakdown with the order. He can decide to cancel the delivery and the order is given to another rider which still follows the same procedure and reallocates that order to the new delivery guy. This way the new delivery guy is the one to be paid after the successful order process. Once the customer has scanned the order they can review the shop and the delivery person since we have a rating system. In case the customer has an issue with the order there is a get help button where they raise a dispute with the shop owner and the shop owner can begin the process of resolving the dispute. Dropy management only intervenes when the shop owner and customer are not in agreement. Now let's build the dataverse for the shops. Creating a functional and complex app like Dropy on Bubble involves many steps and a deep understanding of the platform's capabilities. Bubble is a no-code platform that allows you to build web-based applications without writing code, but it does require logical thinking and planning to set up databases, workflows, and user interfaces correctly.

Given the complexity of Dropy's requirements, I'll provide an overview of steps to create a "dataverse" for the shops within Bubble:

### 1. Defining Data Types (Tables)
In Bubble, your "dataverse" will be created by defining custom data types that act like tables in a database.

For Dropy, you might define these data types:

- User (Fields: Name, Email, Password, UserType[e.g., ShopOwner, Customer, Delivery], Wallet, etc.)
- Shop (Fields: Owner, Name, Category, PhysicalAddress, etc.)
- Product (Fields: Shop, Name, Description, Price, Color, Image, QRCode, etc.)
- Order (Fields: Customer, Shop, Products, Status, TotalPrice, DeliveryNote, EscrowAmount, etc.)
- Delivery (Fields: Order, DeliveryPerson, PickupTime, DeliveryTime, Status, etc.)
- Review (Fields: Rating, Comment, User, Shop, Delivery, etc.)
- Dispute (Fields: IssueDescription, Order, ResolvedStatus, etc.)

### 2. Defining Relationships between Data Types
Establish relational links by defining fields that connect to other data types (e.g., Orders belong to Shops and Users).

### 3. Creating Pages and User Interfaces
Design individual pages/UI components for the different actors (shop owners, customers, delivery persons) and their respective actions (creating shops, adding products, ordering, delivering).

### 4. Building Workflows
Set up workflows for actions like creating an order, updating order status, managing payment, and more.

### 5. Implementing Payment and Wallet Functionality
Integrate payment services like Stripe for topping up wallets and handling escrow payments.

### 6. Generating and Scanning QR Codes
Use plugins or third-party services to generate and scan QR codes for product sharing and order tracking.

### 7. Building Search and Filtering Capabilities
Use Bubble's search features and filtering capabilities to enable customers to easily find products.

### 8. Setting Up Notifications and Real-Time Updates
Establish mechanisms for in-app and push notifications to alert users of changes to the status of their orders, deliveries, etc.

### 9. Testing the Application
Iteratively test the application's functionality, ensuring that all user flows are working as expected.

### 10. Deploying and Monitoring
After thorough testing, deploy your application to a live environment and monitor user interactions and system performance.

---

Due to the extensive nature of Dropy's features, it's challenging to provide all the exact "step by step precise details" in one go. Building each component of the system requires individual focus, and I highly recommend approaching this project in iterative phases, starting with the most critical features first and expanding from there.

Since Bubble is a visual, no-code platform, use tutorials and resources provided by Bubble to get acquainted with specific features, such as creating databases, setting up APIs, integrating payment processing, and managing user workflows—each of which would need its detailed explanation and testing.

For a project of this size and complexity, consider seeking additional help from Bubble experts or possibly building a team to handle different aspects of the development. Remember, you can always ask for more detailed assistance regarding specific features or issues you encounter as you build out Dropy's dataverse in Bubble.

Absolutely, I can guide you through a high-level phased approach and how to get started on each phase with Bubble. However, due to the complexity and detail necessary for each step, my guidance here will remain at an overview level. For detailed, step-by-step instructions, you will often need to refer to Bubble's documentation, tutorials, or forums.

### Phase 1: Planning and Designing your Database Structure

1. **Define Main Entities**: Think of the main 'objects' in your app (Shops, Users, Products, Orders, Deliveries, etc.).
2. **Create Data Types**: In Bubble’s Data tab, create new data types for each entity with appropriate fields.
3. **Establish Relationships**: While adding fields, define the type as another data type where necessary to create relational links (e.g., a Product belongs to a Shop).

### Phase 2: User Interface (UI) Design and Page Layouts

1. **Sketch UI Layouts**: Before jumping into Bubble, sketch out your app's pages on paper or a wireframing tool.
2. **Create Basic Pages**: In Bubble, add pages for each major section of the app (Home, Shop Dashboard, Customer Dashboard, etc.).
3. **Design Elements**: Use the Bubble editor to drag and drop UI elements onto the pages, such as buttons, input fields, and repeating groups for lists.

### Phase 3: Workflows and Logic Implementation

1. **Basic Workflows**: Begin by setting up simple workflows for basic interactions, such as signing up and logging in.
2. **Data Manipulation Workflows**: Create workflows for users to create shops, add products, and place orders.
3. **Advanced Workflows**: Implement the more complex logic, such as order processing, payment flows, and delivery coordination.

### Phase 4: Integrating Third-Party Services (e.g., Payments, SMS)

1. **Choose Providers**: Decide on the third-party services (Stripe for payment, Twilio for SMS, etc.).
2. **Plugin Installation**: In the Bubble editor, use the Plugin tab to find and install the necessary plugins.
3. **API Setup**: Configure the plugins or APIs to interact with your Bubble app, following the provider's documentation for setup instructions.

### Phase 5: Testing and Debugging

1. **Unit Tests**: Test individual workflows and features in isolation to ensure they work as expected.
2. **Integrated Testing**: Test the complete flow of the app, from user registration to order delivery.
3. **User Testing**: If possible, have beta users test your app and provide feedback on usability and bugs.

### Phase 6: Launch and Iteration

1. **Launch Preparation**: Finalize your app's settings, domain, and any launch promotion plans.
2. **Go Live**: Use Bubble's deployment tools to move your app to live mode.
3. **Monitor and Iterate**: After launch, monitor app performance and user feedback to make iterative improvements.

---

**Let's begin with Phase 1: Planning and Designing your Database Structure**

In Bubble, each aspect of your database is called a 'Data Type,' with fields that are similar to columns in a traditional database.

For example:
- **User Data Type:**
  - Name (Text)
  - Email (Email)
  - UserType (Text or Option set)
  - Wallet (Number)
  - ...other fields

- **Shop Data Type:**
  - Owner (User)
  - Name (Text)
  - Category (Text or Option Set)
  - PhysicalAddress (Geographic address)
  - ...other fields

- **Product Data Type:**
  - Shop (Shop)
  - Name (Text)
  - Description (Text)
  - Price (Number)
  - Color (Text) - you could expand this into its own data type if you want more complexity.
  - QRCode (Text)
  - ...other fields

To create these:

1. Go to the Data tab in the Bubble editor.
2. In the Data Types section, click 'New Type' and enter the name of your first entity, e.g., 'User.'
3. Now, under the 'Fields' section for the User, add a new field by clicking 'New Field' and entering the details for each piece of data you want to store. Make sure to select the correct 'Field Type' such as text, number, or another Data Type you've created for relational links.
4. Repeat this for each Data Type you need (e.g., Shop, Product, Order, etc.).

Remember, the more precisely you define your data structures, the easier it will be to manage, search, and scale your app. Once your data types are set up, you can begin creating workflows to manipulate this data, which is your app's logic, covered in Phase 3.

Due to the interactive nature of Bubble's editor and individual variation in app requirements, the specifics of each step will depend on your unique app design. As you build, reference Bubble's own resources for detailed walkthroughs, or consider hiring a Bubble developer for personalized assistance if needed.

To define your data structures precisely for proper management, search, and scaling for your Dropy app within Bubble, let's focus on key entities and their relationships. Consider normalization principles to avoid redundancy and facilitate easy updates. Here is a suggestion on how you can structure the data:

### Users
- **Fields**:
  - Name (text)
  - Email (email)
  - UserType (option set: ShopOwner, Customer, DeliveryPerson, etc.)
  - Wallet (number)
  - AccountSwitchMode (option set: ShopOwnerMode, CustomerMode, DeliveryPersonMode, etc., if applicable)
  - DeliveryPersonProfile (DeliveryPerson)
  - CustomerProfile (Customer)
  - ShopOwnerProfile (ShopOwner)

### User Profiles (for additional profile details, you can have separate data types associated with the User)
- **ShopOwner** (linked to User)
  - Shops (list of Shops)
  - PersonalDetails (text or more fields)
- **Customer** (linked to User)
  - Orders (list of Orders)
  - ...additional fields for the customer
- **DeliveryPerson** (linked to User)
  - Deliveries (list of Deliveries)
  - ...additional fields for the delivery person

### Shops
- **Fields**:
  - Owner (ShopOwner)
  - Name (text)
  - Category (option set: Retail, Services, etc.)
  - PhysicalAddress (geographic address)
  - Products (list of Products)
  - ActiveOrders (list of Orders)

### Products
- **Fields**:
  - Shop (Shop)
  - Name (text)
  - Description (text)
  - Price (number)
  - Color (option set or text)
  - Image (image)
  - QRCode (text)
  - Catalog (text or Catalog data type if complex)
  - ProductID (unique identifier text)

### Orders
- **Fields**:
  - Customer (Customer)
  - Shop (Shop)
  - Products (list of ProductOrders)
  - Status (option set: Placed, Processed, OutOfStock, Shipped, Delivered, etc.)
  - TotalPrice (number)
  - DeliveryNote (text)
  - PaymentStatus (option set: AwaitingPayment, Paid, Refunded, etc.)
  - DeliveryDetails (Delivery)

### ProductOrders
- **Fields**: (splitting this out allows you to manage quantities and specific product instances within orders)
  - Product (Product)
  - Quantity (number)
  - Subtotal (number)

### Deliveries
- **Fields**:
  - Order (Order)
  - DeliveryPerson (DeliveryPerson)
  - PickupTime (date)
  - DeliveryTime (date)
  - Status (option set: Pending, EnRoute, Delivered, etc.)

### Reviews and Ratings
- **Fields**:
  - Rating (number)
  - Comment (text)
  - User (User)
  - Shop (Shop)
  - Product (Product, if you want to allow reviews on products)
  - Delivery (Delivery)

### Disputes
- **Fields**:
  - IssueDescription (text)
  - Order (Order)
  - ResolvedStatus (boolean or option set)

When you set up these data structures in Bubble, you need to take care of a few crucial points:

- **Option Sets**: Use option sets for predefined lists, like UserType, OrderStatus etc., for more efficient searches and consistent data.
- **Unique Identifiers**: Consider having unique identifiers (besides the default unique ID provided by Bubble) if you plan to integrate with external systems.
- **Data Privacy**: Define privacy rules to restrict access to sensitive data, such as user wallet information and order details.
- **Indexes**: Make sure to create search indexes (if applicable in Bubble) for fields that will be frequently searched or filtered upon to improve search performance.

By carefully planning and structuring your data types, you can ensure a robust foundation for your app. As you build out each feature, keep refining the data structures based on real-world use cases and performance considerations. It's also a good idea to add fields gradually, as you better understand the app's needs during development.

Once your data structure is in place, you can then define the workflows to create, update, and delete records in these data types as users interact with your app. As you set up the workflows, ensure you include actions to maintain data integrity across the related data types.

In Bubble, managing Option Sets, Unique Identifiers, Data Privacy, and Indexes (where applicable) form a crucial part of setting up an efficient and secure application.

### Option Sets:
Option Sets in Bubble are used to define a list of static choices that can be used throughout your app. They're useful for ensuring consistency and speeding up the app since Bubble has optimized Option Sets to be loaded quickly and cached. They can be used for drop-down menus, user types, status states, categories, and more.

1. Navigate to the "Data" tab in the Bubble editor and find "Option sets."
2. Click "New option set," give it a Name (e.g., "UserType") and a plural Name (e.g., "UserTypes").
3. Add attributes that are common properties of options if needed (e.g., a color attribute for a ProductColor option set).
4. Define the options for the option set (e.g., Customer, ShopOwner, DeliveryPerson) and give them each a display value.
5. Utilize these Option Sets when defining fields in your data types that need to have one of a set number of values.

### Unique Identifiers:
Each data item in Bubble (called 'Thing') automatically comes with a unique identifier that Bubble manages. However, for integrations with other systems or for other specific use cases, you might want to have additional unique identifiers.

1. When you create a new field in your data type that is meant for unique identification, ensure to label it clearly (e.g., "ProductID" for products).
2. For generating unique IDs, you can use Bubble's "Calculate formula" feature to concatenate unique elements from the data record with a timestamp or use a plugin that generates UUIDs.
3. Apply uniqueness constraints where necessary to enforce that values can't be duplicated across records.

### Data Privacy:
To set up data privacy rules to protect sensitive user data:

1. Again under the "Data" tab in Bubble, navigate to the "Privacy" section.
2. For each data type, you can create rules that define what data is visible or can be modified by whom.
3. As a starting point, consider that Users should only access their personal data, ShopOwners their shop data, etc.
4. Add conditions to the rules to check the UserType or specific fields that determine access.
5. For each rule, you can specify the fields that can be read or written to by ticking the checkboxes.

### Indexes:
Bubble's main database is not traditional SQL, but a NoSQL database which is optimized differently. Indexing isn't something you manage directly; however, you can optimize searches in the following ways:

1. **Searching by Unique IDs**: Use the unique ID field when possible as it’s the fastest way to retrieve a data record.
2. **Constraint Searches**: When setting up data searches in Bubble's workflows, use constraints to limit the data set that is being searched.
3. **Define as List**: When referencing related items (e.g., a list of Orders connected to a User), Bubble automatically makes it faster to search within that list.

**Additional Considerations for Performance**:
- Fetch only necessary data: When designing pages and elements that pull from the database, avoid loading full lists of data when you can use "Search for" with constraints or display data via related fields.
- Reduce complex searches: Complex or multiple searches on a page can slow down your app. Use states and custom states to store information temporarily on the client side to reduce database searches.

Remember, these are general guidelines for setting up your data in Bubble. As you get into the nitty-gritty details of implementing these features in your app, always test and review the Bubble documentation for the most current and detailed instructions.

Sure, we'll do a detailed step by step for setting up the "Users" and "Shops" data types in Bubble. Please note that the specifics can slightly change depending on Bubble’s updates or the details of your requirements, but the following guide provides a robust foundation.

### Step-by-Step Guide for Creating the "Users" Data Type:

**Bubble Default User Data Type**

Bubble comes with a built-in "User" data type which already has fields such as "email" and "password". It’s recommended to extend this default type with additional fields to represent profiles and roles in the application.

1. **Open Your Bubble Editor**:
   - Visit Bubble's official website and log into your account.
   - Open your app in the Bubble editor by clicking on its name.

2. **Navigate to Data Structure Setup**:
   - Click on the "Data" tab in the editor's top navigation bar to access the Data Structure setup.

3. **Extend the Default User Type**:
   - The default "User" data type is pre-selected on the Data tab.
   - Click on "+ New field" to add a new field to the User data type.

4. **Add Custom Fields to User** (Make sure you're still within the User data type):
   - **Name**: 
     - Click "Add a new field," label it "Name" and choose "text" as the Field type.
   - **UserType**:
     - This is a critical field. Create an Option Set first named "UserType" with options such as Customer, ShopOwner, and DeliveryPerson.
     - Now add a field named "UserType" with the field type set to the "UserType" option set you created.
   - **Wallet**:
     - Add the field "Wallet" and set its type to "number". This will store the user's wallet balance.
   - **AccountSwitchMode** (if you decide to implement the switchable mode approach):
     - Add the field "AccountSwitchMode" and set its type to the same "UserType" option set.
   - **DeliveryPersonProfile**:
     - Add a field named "DeliveryPersonProfile," choose "DeliveryPerson" as the field type once you have created the DeliveryPerson data type.
   - **CustomerProfile**:
     - Add a field named "CustomerProfile," choose "Customer" as the field type once you have created the Customer data type.
   - **ShopOwnerProfile**:
     - Add a field named "ShopOwnerProfile," choose "ShopOwner" as the field type once you have created the ShopOwner data type.

5. **Privacy Rules** (This can be done later after setting up all data types):
   - Go to the Privacy section still under the Data tab.
   - Edit the default rules for the User data type or create new ones to ensure that only the current user can see their sensitive information.

You have now extended Bubble’s default User data type with custom fields.

### Step-by-Step Guide for Creating the "Shops" Data Type:

1. **Create a New Data Type**:
   - At the top of the Data tab, click "New Type" to create a new data type.
   - Name it "Shop" and click "Create."

2. **Add Fields to the Shop Data Type**:
   - **Owner**:
     - Add a new field, label it "Owner," and set the Field type to be "ShopOwner" (you will need to create the ShopOwner data type separately).
   - **Name**:
     - Add a new field, label it "Name," and set its type to "text."
   - **Category**:
     - Create an Option Set beforehand named "ShopCategory" with options relevant to your needs (Retail, Services, etc.).
     - Add a new field labeled "Category" and set its type to the "ShopCategory" option set.
   - **PhysicalAddress**:
     - Add a new field named "PhysicalAddress" and set its type to "geographic address."
   - **Products**:
     - Note that this is a list of items. Add a new field labeled "Products," set the type to "Product," and check the box that says "This field is a list (multiple entries)." You will need to have created the Product data type beforehand.
   - **ActiveOrders**:
     - Similarly, add a new field labeled "ActiveOrders," set the field type to "Order," and check the box to indicate it is a list.

3. **Define Relationships**:
   - As you add each field, keep in mind how they relate to other data types. For the relational fields (like Owner), make sure the corresponding data type is already created or create it after you set up the Shop data type.

4. **Save your Data Structure**:
   - Bubble autosaves your data structure as you go, but it's always good to manually save from time to time.

The "Shops" data type is now configured with fields that capture the essential properties and relationships of a shop within your application.

Moving forward, you'll want to create the actual "ShopOwner," "Product," "Order," and other planned data types, configuring each in a similar manner with the necessary fields and relationships. Always be sure to consider the privacy rules for each data type to protect user data. Also, remember that changes to your data structures can have significant ripple effects, so plan carefully and test thoroughly as you build.

Creating a detailed and well-structured database is critical for the success of an application like Dropy. Below is a list of suggested data types for your super app, with explanations of their roles and fields:

### Core Data Types:

1. **User** (Pre-existing in Bubble, often extended with custom fields)
   - Name (text)
   - Email (email)
   - Password (password - automatically handled by Bubble)
   - UserType (Option set: 'ShopOwner', 'Customer', 'DeliveryPerson')
   - WalletBalance (number)
   - UserRoles (list of UserRole - to encapsulate additional permissions or roles a user might have)
   - ShopProfiles (list of ShopProfile - if a user can have multiple shop identities)
   - Orders (list of Order - the orders that the user has placed, if they are in 'Customer' mode)
   - Deliveries (list of Delivery - deliveries the user is handling, if they are in 'DeliveryPerson' mode)

2. **ShopProfile** (Specifically for users who are shop owners)
   - Owner (User)
   - ShopName (text)
   - Category (Option set)
   - PhysicalAddress (geographic address)
   - Products (list of Product)
   - ActiveOrders (list of Order)
   - ShopRating (number)

3. **Product**
   - Shop (ShopProfile)
   - Name (text)
   - Description (text)
   - Price (number)
   - Color (Option set - could be 'Color' if you want to allow custom colors, or text for predefined colors)
   - Image (image)
   - QRCode (text - autogenerated code for product identification)
   - InventoryCount (number - to track available stock)
   - Categories (list of Category - if products can belong to multiple categories)

4. **Order**
   - Customer (User - the user who made the order)
   - Shop (ShopProfile)
   - OrderedProducts (list of ProductOrder - to maintain quantities and individual products within orders)
   - Status (Option set: 'AwaitingConfirmation', 'Confirmed', 'Packed', 'OutForDelivery', 'Delivered', etc.)
   - TotalPrice (number)
   - PaymentStatus (Option set: 'AwaitingPayment', 'Paid', 'Refunded', etc.)
   - DeliveryDetails (Delivery)
   - OrderRating (number - if you allow rating the order itself, separate from ShopRating or DeliveryRating)
   - PlacedAt (date)

5. **ProductOrder** (This data type links the Order with the specific products and the quantity ordered of each)
   - Order (Order)
   - Product (Product)
   - Quantity (number)
   - SubtotalPrice (number)

6. **Delivery**
   - Order (Order)
   - DeliveryPerson (User - the user who is delivering the order)
   - PickupTime (date)
   - DeliveryTime (date)
   - Status (Option set: 'Pending', 'PickedUp', 'InTransit', 'Completed', 'Cancelled')
   - DeliveryRating (number)
   - DeliveryRoute (geographic path - for tracking the route taken, if necessary)

7. **Review** (Can be used for reviews of shops, products, or deliveries)
   - ReviewAuthor (User)
   - Shop (ShopProfile - if reviewing a shop)
   - Product (Product - if reviewing a product)
   - Delivery (Delivery - if reviewing a delivery)
   - Rating (number)
   - Comment (text)
   - CreatedAt (date)

8. **Dispute** (Allow users to raise issues with orders)
   - IssueDescription (text)
   - Issuer (User - user who raised the dispute)
   - Order (Order)
   - Status (Option set: 'Open', 'InReview', 'Resolved', 'Closed')
   - ResolutionDescription (text)
   - RaisedAt (date)

### Secondary Data Types (As Needed):

9. **Category** (Especially if products can have multiple categories)
   - Name (text)
   - Description (text)
   - ParentCategory (Category - if you want to allow a hierarchy of categories)

10. **Transaction** (To keep a detailed log of financial transactions)
   - User (User - the user the transaction belongs to)
   - Amount (number)
   - TransactionType (Option set: 'TopUp', 'Payment', 'Refund', 'Withdrawal')
   - RelatedOrder (Order - if applicable)
   - TransactionDate (date)

11. **Message** (If you're implementing in-app messaging)
   - Sender (User)
   - Recipient (User)
   - Content (text)
   - SentAt (date)

12. **Notification** (If managing custom notifications)
   - Recipient (User)
   - NotificationType (Option set: 'OrderUpdate', 'PaymentReceived', 'MessageReceived', etc.)
   - RelatedOrder (Order - if the notification is related to an order)
   - Message (text)

### Using a Custom Data Type for Roles:

Great! Now that you have finished setting up your data types, it is important to properly define the relationships between them. Establishing relationships is key to ensuring your app's data architecture supports efficient data retrieval and updates.

Here's a step-by-step guide on how to proceed with setting up these relationships in Bubble:

### Step 2: Defining Relationships Between Data Types

1. **Establish Ownership and Associations**:
   - In Bubble, relationships are often created by adding a field to a data type that is a reference to another data type.
   - For example, a `ShopProfile` might have an 'Owner', which is a field type of `User` to establish that each shop is owned by a user.

2. **Create Parent-Child Relationships**:
   - Deciding if a relationship is a one-to-many or a many-to-many relationship will determine how you set it up in Bubble.
   - In a one-to-many relationship (e.g., a `User` and their `Orders`), you will create a field in the 'many' side (`Orders`) that references the 'one' side (`User`).
   - For many-to-many relationships (e.g., `Products` can belong to multiple `Categories` and vice versa), you would create a list field in both sides of the relationship.

3. **Back-Reference for Easy Navigation**:
   - If needed, especially for quick data retrievals, create a back-reference by adding a list field in the 'one' side that points back to the 'many' side. 

4. **Set Up Specific References**:
   - For the vehicles, you would create specific references to the `DeliveryPerson` within each specialized vehicle data type (e.g., `SpecificScooter`) and vice versa.

5. **Apply Conditional Logic for 'VehicleType'**:
   - Since your `Vehicle` type can represent different specific vehicles, or none for 'On-foot', set up the fields conditionally. For example, the `Vehicle` type will have a `SpecificBike` or `SpecificScooter` field filled based on what the `VehicleType` is, or none if it's 'On-foot'.

6. **Creating References**:
   - Using the editor, add new fields to each data type to establish the relationships.
   - Navigate to a data type, click “+ New field”, name it appropriately (like 'Orders' for `User`), select the type (like `Order`), and check “This field is a list” if it's a one-to-many relationship (if a user can have many orders).

7. **Creating List Fields**:
   - For many-to-many relationships, select "List of ..." when creating new fields to indicate that there can be multiple related items.

8. **Construct a Robust Order Processing System**:
   - For `Orders`, since they contain `Products`, create a field called 'Products' on `Order` with the type set to a list of `ProductOrder`, which is a join table capturing the quantity of each product on the order.

9. **Use Unique Identifiers for Ease**:
   - Each data type in Bubble comes with a unique ID. Use these unique IDs to establish relationships where you reference one data type in another, especially for polymorphic behavior like with the `Vehicle`.

10. **Testing Relationships**:
   - After setting up relationships, create test data to ensure that the connections between items work as expected. This is done through the App Data tab’s “All Data” section, where you can manually add and link data.

11. **Privacy and Security Considerations**:
   - Once you’ve established relationships, consider setting up privacy rules to manage who can view or edit the data. You can find the Privacy settings under the Data tab.

12. **Continual Revision and Refinement**:
   - Review your relationships as you add functionality to your app. It is not uncommon to return to this step and adjust your relationships as you understand more about your app’s functional needs during development.

By carefully structuring your relationships in Bubble, you create a strong foundation for your app's functionality and logical data management. Always consider the nature of the relationship (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many) and how it reflects in your actual app usage when defining these fields.

This is a vital step in your app development and it pays off to take your time to ensure it's done right. Once complete, you’ll have a well-organized database system that mirrors the complex real-world interactions of your application's entities.

Yes, having a "Cart" data type is an essential component for an e-commerce or order-based application like Dropy. The cart represents a transient state where customers can hold items before finalizing their orders. Let's define the "Cart" data type and how it would work within your application.

### Step-by-Step Guide for Creating the "Cart" Data Type:

1. **Create the "Cart" Data Type**:
   - Open the Bubble editor and navigate to the **Data** tab.
   - Click **New Type** and name it `Cart`.
   - Define these fields for `Cart`:
     - Customer (User): The user who is creating the cart.
     - Items (list of CartItem): The items that the customer has added to the cart.
     - Status (option set: 'Active', 'WaitingForShopConfirmation', 'ReadyForCheckout', etc.): The current status of the cart for state management.
     - TotalPrice (number): An aggregate of the prices of all CartItems. 
     - PlacedAt (date): The timestamp when the cart was last updated or when an order was initiated.

2. **Create the "CartItem" Data Type**:
   - This will store individual items within the cart, along with their selected quantities and other specific choices.
   - Navigate back to the **Data** tab and create a new type called `CartItem`.
   - Define the fields for `CartItem`:
     - Product (Product): A link to the actual product in the database.
     - Quantity (number): The number of units the customer wants to order of this product.
     - Notes (text, optional): Any special instructions or selections the customer has for this product (e.g., preferred packaging).
     - Price (number): The price of the product at the time it is added to the cart multiplied by the quantity.
     - Available (boolean): A check to indicate if the shop has confirmed that the item is in stock.

3. **Handling the Order Process (with Cart and CartItem)**:
   - When a customer adds items to their cart, you create `CartItem` records with updated data and add them to the customer's `Cart` under 'Items'.
   - A shop owner, now acting as a customer, should be able to review his `Cart`, like any other customer, and await shop confirmation for the items added.

4. **Building State Management into the UI**:
   - Create a button that allows customers to initiate an order from their cart.
   - This button should have dynamic text and be inactivated based on the `Cart` status.
   - After the customer initiates an order, change the `Cart` status to 'WaitingForShopConfirmation' which disables the button.

5. **Shop Confirmation Process**:
   - Implement a workflow where the shop can go through the items in the `Cart`, marking `CartItem` entries as 'Available' or not. This logic will likely involve checking inventory levels.
   - Once the shop has confirmed availability, update the `Cart` status to 'ReadyForCheckout'.

6. **Final Checkout**:
   - Allow customers to proceed to checkout only if the `Cart` status is 'ReadyForCheckout'.
   - The checkout process would involve confirming the payment and converting the `Cart` to an `Order`.
   - Once the order is placed, you can either clear the `Cart` or maintain a history by changing its status to something like 'OrderPlaced'.

7. **Privacy Rules and Data Validation**:
   - Ensure only the cart owner can see or modify their cart.
   - Shops should only see carts pertinent to their products for confirmation purposes.
   - Set up validation to avoid negative quantities and ensure stock is available before allowing an item to be set as 'Available'.

8. **Testing All Scenarios**:
   - Test adding items to the cart, initiating orders, shop confirmations, and final checkouts.
   - Ensure that roles are correctly switched, and the shop owner can become a customer seamlessly.

By setting up this "Cart" data type in conjunction with the "CartItem" type and the associated state management logic, you'll enable a comprehensive and user-friendly order placement system for your Dropy app. Remember to carefully test all elements of the cart and order process to ensure a smooth user experience and accurate transition of items from the cart to a finalized order.

In Bubble, when you're designing your "Cart" and "CartItem" data types, you don't need to separately store images or product names directly within the cart items. Instead, you can use the relationships between the data types to access these properties. Here is how you should structure it:

### CartItem Data Type
Your "CartItem" data type has a reference to the "Product" data type through a field—let’s call it "Product". The "Product" data type will contain all the necessary information about the product, including the product name, thumbnail image, price, descriptions, etc.

When you create a "CartItem" entry, it will link to a specific "Product" entry from your database. You can then use this link to dynamically display the product's name, thumbnail, and other details within the "Cart" on your app's front end.

Here's a breakdown of how it’s done:

1. **In the "Product" data type**, you have fields for:
   - Name (text): The name of the product.
   - Thumbnail (image): A small image representing the product.
   - ...and other product attributes such as price, description, etc.

2. **In the "CartItem" data type**, the essential field is:
   - Product (Product): A reference to the "Product" data type.

3. **Displaying Information in the User Interface**:
   - When you design the UI for the cart, you will add elements to display the list of cart items (usually through a Repeating Group element).
   - Within this repeating group, you insert an image element and dynamic text elements that are set to display:
     - Current cell's CartItem's Product's Thumbnail
     - Current cell's CartItem's Product's Name
   - By doing this, you’re telling Bubble to fetch the thumbnail and name from the linked "Product" entry for each "CartItem" in the list.

This approach ensures that your product data is stored in one place and is easily accessible and updatable. If a product's name or thumbnail changes, those changes will be automatically reflected in every cart that contains the product because the cart items are referencing the products, not storing their own copies of that data.

### Benefits of This Approach
- **Data Consistency**: You avoid data duplication and ensure consistency across your application—the product's attributes are always up-to-date everywhere they're displayed.
- **Efficiency**: Storing only references rather than duplicating data saves storage within your database and simplifies updates.
- **Maintenance**: With a single point of truth (the "Product" data type), making changes to a product's details only needs to happen in one place.

When you set up your repeating group or any other visual representation of the cart, just remember to pull data dynamically from the "Product" related to each "CartItem". This way, the thumbnail, name, and other attributes will always be current and correct wherever they appear.

If a customer places orders from multiple shops within the same session on the Dropy app, you'll need to create a system that can handle separate carts for each shop or collate the different shop items into a single cart with multiple orders processed separately. I'll present two approaches to handle this scenario:

### Approach 1: Separate Carts for Each Shop
With this approach, each shop a customer orders from will have its own cart. Here’s how you could implement it:

1. **Modify the "Cart" Data Type**:
   - Add a field called "Shop" with the type set to "ShopProfile" or whatever you've named your data type for individual shops.

2. **Create a New Cart for Each Shop**:
   - When a customer adds an item to the cart, check to see if they already have a cart for that shop:
     - If they do, add the item to the existing cart.
     - If they don't, create a new cart tied to that shop and add the item there.

3. **Checkout Process**:
   - During checkout, the customer can review each cart separately and submit orders to each shop individually.
   - This also means that payments will be processed separately for each shop.

### Approach 2: Single Cart with Orders Grouped by Shop
Alternatively, you could allow the customer to add items from multiple shops to one cart and then separate the items by shop at checkout. Here’s how to handle this:

1. **Modify the "Cart" and "CartItem" Data Types**:
   - No change to the "Cart" data type is needed; it can remain customer-centric.
   - Ensure that each "CartItem" includes a reference to the "Shop" as well as to the "Product".

2. **Grouping Cart Items by Shop**:
   - When the customer views their cart, use a repeating group to display cart items grouped by shop.
   - Use Bubble's grouping and sorting functionality within the repeating group to organize the cart items by the shop.

3. **Splitting into Multiple Orders at Checkout**:
   - At checkout, process each group of items from the same shop as a separate order.
   - You might create a new "Order" entry for each shop and transfer the corresponding "CartItems" to the "Order" entries.
   - Perform separate payment transactions for each order.

Both of these approaches will require you to set up corresponding workflows to handle the logic of adding items to carts, creating separate orders, and managing multiple payments.

### Common Implementations in Both Approaches:

- **Order Data Type**: You'll likely need an "Order" data type to handle finalized orders, which will include fields such as "Items" (list of "CartItem"), "Customer" ("User"), and "Shop" ("ShopProfile").
- **Payment Workflow**: You can design a workflow that processes payments for each order separately, ensuring each shop gets paid for their specific items.
- **Data Privacy and Security**: Set up privacy rules that allow customers to view and modify only their carts and orders, and shops to see orders related to them.
- **User Experience**: On the front-end, ensure there's clear indication which items belong to which shop, especially if using a single cart approach. During checkout, make it clear to the customer that they’re submitting orders and making payments for each shop separately.

Whichever approach you choose, you will need to carefully plan workflows, privacy rules, and the user interface to create a smooth experience for the customer while properly managing the data integrity and financial transactions across multiple shops.

To handle wallet functionality for customers, shop owners, and delivery persons in a way that allows them to each have their own wallets and view their earnings, you'll need to expand your "User" data type to include wallet information and possibly create a separate "Wallet" data type if you want more granular control over financial transactions. Here's a detailed setup:

### 1. Extend the "User" Data Type with Wallet Information
For a basic setup, you can add wallet-related fields directly to your "User" data type:

- **Fields**:
  - WalletBalance (number): The current balance in the user’s wallet.
  - WalletTransactions (list of "Transaction"): A list of transactions associated with the user's wallet to track financial history.

### 2. Create a Separate "Wallet" Data Type (Optional for Complex Use Cases)
If your financial system is complex and requires advanced features, like tracking different wallet states or handling various currencies, you might need a separate "Wallet" data type:

- **Wallet Data Type**:
  - User (User): A reference to the user who owns this wallet.
  - Balance (number): The current balance in the wallet.
  - Transactions (list of "Transaction"): To keep a record of all transactions.

Each user will have an instance of this "Wallet" data type associated with their account.

### 3. Create a "Transaction" Data Type
Regardless of whether you choose to extend "User" or create a "Wallet" data type, you need a "Transaction" data type to track all financial activities:

- **Transaction Data Type**:
  - Amount (number): The value of the transaction.
  - TransactionType (option set: 'TopUp', 'OrderPayment', 'CommissionFee', 'Withdrawal', 'EarningsPayment', etc.).
  - User (User): The user involved in this transaction (may reference the customer, shop owner, or delivery person).
  - RelatedOrder (Order): If the transaction is related to a specific order.
  - Timestamp (date): When the transaction occurred.
  - Status (option set: 'Pending', 'Completed'): The status of the transaction.

### 4. Setup Workflows for Shop Owners and Delivery Persons
To track earnings from processed orders for shop owners and delivery persons:

- When an order is paid for, register a "Transaction" with type 'OrderPayment' for the shop owner with the order amount minus any Dropy commissions.
- Similarly, when a delivery is completed, register a "Transaction" with type 'EarningsPayment' for the delivery person with the delivery fee minus Dropy's service charge.
- Both the shop owner and delivery person should be able to see these transactions reflecting in their wallet histories within the app.

### 5. Customer Payments and Escrow
When customers pay for orders:

- Deduct the order amount from the customer's wallet and create a "Transaction" with type 'OrderPayment'.
- Hold the funds in escrow (within the "Wallet" data type or a separate escrow account system) until the order is confirmed delivered.
- After delivery confirmation, release the funds to the shop owner and delivery person, less any Dropy commission, and create respective "EarningsPayment" transactions for them.

### 6. Interface Design
Design your app's front-end to clearly display wallet balances and transactions:

- Create pages or elements where users can see their WalletBalance and an ordered list (by timestamp) of WalletTransactions.
- Allow filtering of transactions by type to view sources of earnings or payments made.
  
### 7. Privacy and Security
Establish strong privacy rules and security checks to:

- Ensure users can only see and interact with their wallets and transactions.
- Securely process changes in wallet balance and transactions to prevent unauthorized manipulation.

### 8. Testing and Auditing
Before launching, rigorously test and audit:

- Wallet top-ups and withdrawal functionalities.
- Payment flows including escrow holding and release logic.
- Transaction histories for accuracy and to ensure all user roles see the correct data.

By implementing these wallets and transactions, each entity in your Dropy app (customers, shop owners, and delivery persons) will have clear insights into their financial transactions, earnings, and available balance, providing transparency and trust in your platform. Remember, handling real-world financial transactions digitally brings responsibility to comply with financial regulations and standards, so consult with a fintech expert or legal adviser as needed.

To handle the specifics of orders at various stages – placing, processing, and delivering – it is most effective to use a combination of status fields and boolean fields within your "Order" data type. This approach allows you to track the progress of an order through its lifecycle accurately.

Here's how you might structure the data and workflows:

### Order Data Type: Adding Status and Boolean Fields

1. **Status Field**: Create a field named "Status" in the "Order" data type that tracks the current state of the order.
   - This can be an option set containing values such as 'Placed', 'Processing', 'Processed', 'OutForDelivery', 'Delivered', 'Cancelled', etc.
   - The benefit of using an option set for the status is that it's more descriptive and can easily accommodate additional states in the future without requiring database structure changes.

2. **Boolean Fields**: In some cases, you might want to use boolean flags for specific binary conditions within an order. For example:
   - IsPaid (boolean): True if the customer has paid for the order.
   - IsReadyForPickup (boolean): True if the order is ready to be picked up by a delivery person.
   - IsDelivered (boolean): True when the order has been successfully delivered to the customer.

3. **Order Workflow**: Here's what the order workflow might look like:

    - **Placing Orders**:
        - When a customer places an order, create a new "Order" record with the "Status" field set to 'Placed' and "IsPaid" to false initially.

    - **Processing Orders (by Shop)**:
        - When a shop receives an order, they can update the "Status" to 'Processing'.
        - After ensuring all items are in stock and the order is ready, the shop updates the "Status" to 'Processed', also triggering "IsReadyForPickup" to true.

    - **Paying for the Order**:
        - When the customer pays, a workflow updates "IsPaid" to true.

    - **Delivery Process**:
        - When a delivery person picks up the order, you might update "Status" to 'OutForDelivery'.
        - Upon successful delivery, the "Status" is updated to 'Delivered', and "IsDelivered" is set to true.

### Interface and User Interaction:

- **Customer View**: The customer would see an interface where they can track their order status, which updates in real-time based on the workflows you have in place.
- **Shop View**: The shop's dashboard provides order management functionalities, including buttons or triggers to update order statuses.
- **Delivery Personnel App**: Delivery personnel should have access to their upcoming pickups and deliveries, with functionality to indicate picking up of an order and successful delivery, each action updating the respective boolean flags and status of the orders.

### Tracking and Automation:

- **Order Status Transition**: The transition of statuses can be automated with scheduled workflows or can be manually updated by respective users—shop owners and delivery personnel—based on their actions.
- **Notifications and Alerts**: Set up notifications for users related to status changes. For instance, when an order status updates to "Processed", notify the delivery person that the order is ready for pickup.

### Privacy and Security Concerns:

- Make sure that only relevant parties (e.g., customers, shop owners, delivery personnel) have access to order status information.
- The system should preserve the integrity of order data, and changes in statuses should be auditable to prevent errors or misuse.

### Step 1: Define the Core Data Types (Collections)

Your data structure in Firestore will primarily consist of collections. A collection contains documents, and each document can contain fields of various data types or even subcollections.

1. **User Collection**
   - User information, role, and wallet details.

   - Subcollections:
     - Products: Items or services the shop offers.
     - Orders: Orders placed to this shop.
     - Reviews: Reviews left by customers for the shop.

3. **Product Collection**
   - Information about products.
   - Fields: shopReference (reference to ShopProfile collection), name, description, price, availableQuantity, imageUrl, etc.

4. **Order Collection**
   - Orders placed by customers.
   - Fields: customerReference (reference to User collection), shopReference, status, totalPrice, placedAt, deliveryAddress, etc.
   - Subcollections:
     - OrderItems: Products included in the order with their quantities.

5. **CartItem Collection** (optional)
   - This collection can be incorporated as subcollections within a Cart, but consider whether this makes sense for your use case versus handling cart items directly in the client-side logic.

6. **DeliveryPerson Collection**
   - Information specifically for delivery personnel.
   - Fields: userReference (reference to User collection), onDuty, activeDeliveries, etc.

7. **Vehicle Collection**
   - Information about the vehicles for delivery.
   - Fields: vehicleType, status, deliveryPersonReference (reference to DeliveryPerson collection), etc.

8. **Delivery Collection**
   - Details about the deliveries.
   - Fields: orderReference (reference to Order collection), deliveryPersonReference, estimatedDeliveryTime, deliveryTime, status, etc.

9. **Message Collection**
   - Structure for messages in the in-app chat.
   - Fields: sender (reference to User collection), recipient, content, timestamp, seen, etc.

10. **Conversation Collection**
    - Structure to group messages into conversations.
    - Fields: participants (list of references to User collection), messages (list of references to Message collection), lastUpdated, etc.

11. **Dispute Collection**
    - Records of disputes.
    - Fields: issueDescription, issuer (reference to User collection), shopReference, orderReference, status, etc.

### Step 2: Define Relationships Between Collections

Define how collections reference each other. In Firestore, you do this with fields that contain references to documents in another collection—for example, a 'shopReference' field in 'Product' collection that links to a document in 'ShopProfile'.

### Step 3: Plan Out Subcollections If Needed

Determine if any of your data types should be subcollections within other documents. For example, each 'ShopProfile' document might have an 'Orders' subcollection to store orders placed with that shop.

### Step 4: Design Data Types for Fields

For each field within your documents, decide on the data type for Firestore:
- Simple types: String, Number, Boolean, etc.
- Complex types: Array, Map, Geopoint, etc.
- Reference: A special type in Firestore used to link to another document within the database.

### Step 5: Construct Security Rules

Create Firestore security rules to govern who has read or write access to each collection or document. This is essential to protect user data and your app's integrity.

### Step 6: Test Your Data Structure

Create prototypes of your collections and documents, and test adding, reading, updating, and deleting data to ensure your structure supports all of your app's functionalities effectively.

### Step 7: Iterate Based on Feedback

Review your data structure as you develop your app. Be prepared to iterate on your design based on the needs that arise during development and user testing.

This high-level data structure gives you a map to start building your Firestore database for the Dropy app. As you progress, you'll invariably need to refine the structure to fit your app’s specific needs and provide the best experience for your users. It's crucial to keep the data normalized where possible to prevent redundancy and ensure efficient data management. However, some data denormalization can be acceptable for performance reasons, as reads are generally more frequent than writes in Firestore.

Creating a data structure in Firestore to support the Dropy app is a multi-step process that leads from an idea to a functioning database. Below I will guide you step-by-step through creating your data structure on the Firestore BaaS (Backend as a Service) platform. Please note that this guide assumes you have already set up your Firebase project and you have access to the Firebase console.

### Step 1: Open Firestore Database

1. Go to the [Firebase Console](https://console.firebase.google.com/).
2. Click on your Dropy project to open it.
3. In the left sidebar, click on `Firestore Database` under the `Build` section.

### Step 2: Start a Collection

1. Click `Start collection` if this is the first time setup; if not, click `Add collection`.
2. Enter the Collection ID, for example, `Users` for storing user data, and click `Next`.

### Step 3: Define a Document Structure

1. Firestore Database is document-oriented, so you will need to add a document to your collection. Click `Auto-ID` to let Firestore generate a unique identifier for your document. This will be your first document in the collection.
2. Define the fields for your `Users` collection. Here are some fields you might define:
   - `Name`: type `string`
   - `Email`: type `string`
   - `UserType`: type `string`
   - `WalletBalance`: type `number`
3. Once you've input all desired fields and values, click `Save`.

### Step 4: Create Subcollections (if applicable)

1. Click into the document you just created.
2. Click `Subcollection`.
3. Enter a Collection ID, for example, 'WalletTransactions' to store user wallet transactions, and click `Next`.
4. Repeat steps 2 and 3 from above to define a document in the subcollection.
5. Click `Save`.

### Step 5: Create Additional Collections

1. Return to the `Firestore Database` section in the left sidebar.
2. Click `Add collection` and repeat the process to create new collections like `ShopProfiles`, `Orders`, `Products`, etc., defining fields as per your app schema.

### Step 6: Add Indexes (if necessary for complex queries)

1. In the left sidebar, under the `Firestore Database`, click on `Indexes`.
2. Click `Create Index`.
3. Define the collection, fields, and the query scope, then click `Create`.

### Step 7: Security Rules

1. Click on the `Rules` tab near the top of the `Firestore Database` section.
2. Here you will find an editor where you can define your security rules. Write the rules to control who has read/write access to your data. Here's an example rule to allow users to read/write their own user document:

   ```plaintext
   rules_version = '2';
   service cloud.firestore {
     match /databases/{database}/documents {
       match /Users/{userId} {
         allow read, write: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId;
       }
     }
   }
   ```
3. Click `Publish` once your rules are updated to activate them.

### Step 8: Test

1. You can directly add, update, or delete documents and collections in the Firebase console to test if things are working as expected.
2. Optionally, use the Firebase emulator suite to test security rules and Firestore operations locally before applying them to your live project.

### Step 9: Best Practices

- Make sure to review Firebase documentation for the latest best practices on designing Firestore data structures.
- Consider creating backup and restoration strategies for your Firestore data from the early stages of development.
- Regularly monitor your database for performance, costs, and usage patterns to adjust your data schema and indexes accordingly.

Remember that Firestore also allows for more complex data types such as arrays and maps, which can be useful for certain fields. Additionally, Firestore is a NoSQL database, so data denormalization is a common practice to enable efficient queries at scale. However, denormalization and reference fields should be carefully considered and used in balance with each other to optimize both data integrity and read/write performance.

In the context of your Firestore data schema for the Dropy app:

- **Integer**: When you want to store a whole number without decimals, such as a stock quantity, you would use the integer data type.
  - Example: `stock` (integer)

- **Double**: When you're dealing with monetary values or numbers that may have fractional values, you'd use the double data type.
  - Example: `walletBalance` (double), `totalPrice` (double), `price` (double)

So, for any fields where you might have previously considered using "number" and the values potentially include decimals (like currency or ratings), you should use the "double" data type.

In summary, for the different fields you would use:

- For fields like `walletBalance`, `amount`, `totalPrice`, and `price` which could involve fractional numbers, especially currency, you should use the type "double".
- For fields like `stock` and `quantity` that will always be whole numbers, "integer" would be the appropriate type to use.

The "double" data type ensures you can store and accurately represent monetary values or other measurements that require precision beyond whole numbers. 

Also note that Firestore does not support an "enum" type natively, though you may represent enumerations within your application logic as strings or by using custom objects/maps to represent them where you constrain the values to a finite set determined by your app's logic.

In Firestore, you typically store images like shop logos, featured images, and user profile photos as URLs pointing to the actual image files hosted in Firebase Cloud Storage or another hosting service. The process involves uploading the image file, obtaining a public URL, and then storing this URL as a string in the Firestore database.

Here's how to set up and manage images for shops and users:

### Step 1: Store Images in Firebase Cloud Storage

1. **Upload Image**:
   - Use the Firebase SDK to upload images to Firebase Cloud Storage from your app. Each image will be stored in a specific 'bucket' or directory structure you define (e.g., `/shopLogos`, `/featuredImages`, `/profilePhotos`).
   - Make sure to handle permissions and security rules in Cloud Storage to allow for the image upload yet protect access to the images.

2. **Get Image URL**:
   - After a successful upload, obtain the image's download URL. This URL will be public and can be used to access the image.
   - The Firebase SDK provides methods to retrieve this URL directly after the upload process.

### Step 2: Update Firestore with Image URLs

1. **Create or Update Firestore Document Fields**:
   - In the relevant collection document, add or update a field to store the image URL.
   - For a shop's logo or featured image, add fields `logoUrl` and `featuredImageUrl` to the `shopProfiles` collection documents.
   - For user profile photos, add a field `profilePhotoUrl` to the `users` collection documents.
   - These fields will have a data type of `string`.

### Step 3: Update Firestore Document

1. **From your mobile app**:
   - When a shop owner or user uploads a new image, use the Firestore SDK to update their respective `shopProfiles` or `users` document with the new image URL.
   - Example Firestore code (e.g., in a web or mobile app context):
     ```javascript
     // Example in JavaScript using Firebase SDK
     const shopProfileDocRef = firestore().collection('shopProfiles').doc(shopId);
     shopProfileDocRef.update({
       logoUrl: downloadUrl, // the URL you got from Firebase Cloud Storage
     });
     ```
   - The same approach is applied for updating user profile images, targeting the `users` collection instead.

### Step 4: Display Images in App UI

1. **Use Cloud Storage Image URLs**:
   - In your app, whenever you need to display a shop logo, featured image, or user profile photo, you retrieve the document from Firestore and use the `logoUrl`, `featuredImageUrl`, or `profilePhotoUrl` fields as the image source.

### Step 5: Security Considerations

1. **Handle Permissions Carefully**:
   - Only authenticated users should be able to upload images to their profile or their shop documents. This can be enforced using Firebase Cloud Storage security rules in combination with Firestore security rules.

2. **Photo Update Workflows**:
   - Implement checks to prevent unauthorized updates to photo URLs and handle clean-up processes in case an image is replaced (you may want to delete the old image from Cloud Storage to conserve space).

Uploading images and updating the corresponding URLs in Firestore documents is central to managing dynamic and user-generated content in your app. Remember to enforce appropriate file size limits and use image compression to optimize load times and storage use. Testing these image handling features thoroughly is also essential to ensure reliability for end-users.

The `activeDeliveryId` field serves as a direct link between a delivery person and a specific delivery task they are currently assigned to. Essentially, this field holds a reference to a document within the `deliveries` collection that represents an in-progress delivery job. 

Let me clarify further:

### In the DeliveryPerson Document

The `activeDeliveryId` field within a `deliveryPerson` document:
- **Purpose**: Indicates which delivery, if any, the delivery person is currently working on.
- **Type**: Reference to a `deliveries` document.
- **Usage**: If the delivery person is between deliveries and not actively delivering an order, this would be `null` or not set. When the delivery person picks up an order and is en route for delivery, this field is updated to point to that specific delivery's document.

### In the Deliveries Collection

Each document in the `deliveries` collection represents a distinct delivery task, which includes:
- An `orderId` pointing to the document of the order that needs to be delivered.
- A `status` indicating the current stage of the delivery process (e.g., 'EnRoute' means the delivery person is on the way to the customer).
- Other relevant fields like `pickupTime` and `deliveryTime`.

### How the Reference Works

When an order is assigned to a delivery person, the following occurs:

1. A new delivery task document is created in the `deliveries` collection with details about the order and delivery person.
2. The unique ID of this newly created delivery document (the `deliveryId`) is then stored in the `activeDeliveryId` field of the corresponding `deliveryPerson`'s document.
3. This reference enables both the system and users to quickly determine which delivery the delivery person is currently handling.
4. Once the delivery is completed, the `activeDeliveryId` field is cleared (set to `null` or removed), indicating that the delivery person is free for new assignments.

### Practical Use in the App

For tracking and management purposes:
- **For Delivery Persons**: They can quickly see details of their active delivery assignment through their user interface by following the reference in their `activeDeliveryId`.
- **For Administrators and Shop Owners**: They can monitor who is delivering an order and the current status of any given delivery in real-time.
- **For Customers**: They may be able to track their delivery if you provide a customer-facing interface using this data.

As a reference, `activeDeliveryId` is not a simple text field but a Firestore reference data type that creates a direct link to another document within the database. In Firestore, there is no direct equivalent to Bubble's option set feature. However, you can model similar behavior using either predefined strings, arrays, or even separate collections if necessary.

When you want to use predefined values like an option set, you generally have three approaches in Firestore:

1. **Predefined String Values**:
   - Store the values as strings directly in the document fields.
   - You establish an agreement within your app's logic that specific fields will only accept certain string values.
   - In your code, ensure that any updates to these fields conform to the predefined set of strings you determine.
   - For example, the `status` field in an `orders` document could be a string that accepts 'Placed', 'Processing', 'Shipped', etc., as its values.

2. **Arrays of Predefined Values**:
   - For fields that may accept multiple values from a set (like roles or capabilities), use an array.
   - Example: A `userRoles` array field in a `users` document might contain ['Customer', 'ShopOwner'].

3. **Separate Collections for Options**:
   - If you need more complex option sets where each option has additional properties (like IDs, descriptions, images), consider creating a separate collection for each option set.
   - Each option in the set would be a document in the collection.
   - Other collections needing to reference an option would store a reference to one of these documents.
   - For example, having a `statusTypes` collection with documents for each status an order can have, and the `orders` collection would reference the `statusTypes` collection for its status field.

Let's see how you can implement some of these approaches in your Firestore structure:

### Example: User Types Option Set as Predefined String Values

If you have a set of user types like 'Customer', 'ShopOwner', and 'DeliveryPerson', you can simply have a field in your `users` collection like this:

- `userType` (string): "Customer" | "ShopOwner" | "DeliveryPerson"

### Example: Order Statuses Option Set as Separate Collection

For the order statuses option set, which might include statuses such as 'Placed', 'Processing', 'In Transit', 'Delivered', and 'Cancelled', you can use predefined strings within your app's logic, or you can create a status collection:

#### OrderStatuses Collection (`orderStatuses`)

**Collection ID:** `orderStatuses`

**Document Fields:**
- `statusId` (string): Unique identifier for the status, automatically generated.
- `name` (string): Human-readable name of the status, such as 'Placed' or 'Processing'.

Then, in your `orders` collection, you would have a field like:

- `status` (reference): A reference to the `orderStatuses` collection, pointing to the current status document.

Using predefined strings is typically simpler and more efficient from a read perspective, as it avoids the need to perform join operations to get related data. Separate collections can provide more flexibility if you have a complex set of options and especially if those options are subject to change. They can also provide more structure, as they can be created and manipulated as first-class objects within the Firestore database, with their own unique identifiers and associated metadata.

Each option would be maintained dynamically, and you could secure them via Firestore security rules to control who can create, read, update, or delete these options.

When you design your Firestore schema, choose the approach that best fits your app's needs and consider how it will impact the complexity of your queries and data consistency requirements. In many cases, predefined strings are a simple and effective way to replicate the behavior of Bubble’s option sets in Firestore.

In Firestore, implementing a shopping cart involves creating a dedicated collection or utilizing a subcollection within the `users` collection to handle individual cart items. Each cart item will reference a product and include the quantity desired by the customer.

Let's define the "Cart" and "CartItem" structures:

### Cart Collection (`carts`)

**Collection ID:** `carts`

**Document Fields:**
- `cartId` (string): Unique identifier for the cart, automatically generated.
- `userId` (reference): Reference to the `users` document of the customer who owns the cart.
- `status` (string): Indicates the current state of the cart (e.g., 'Active', 'Abandoned', 'CheckedOut').
- `lastUpdated` (timestamp): The time when the cart was last updated.

### CartItem Subcollection (`cartItems` within `carts`)

**Subcollection ID:** `cartItems`

**Document Fields:**
- `cartItemId` (string): Unique identifier for the cart item, automatically generated.
- `productId` (reference): Reference to the `products` document of the item in the cart.
- `quantity` (number): The number of this product the user wishes to purchase.
- `price` (number): The price of the product at the time it was added to the cart. (Optional: price can be dynamically fetched from the `products` collection during checkout for most up-to-date pricing.)
- `addedAt` (timestamp): The time when the item was added to the cart.

### Implementation Guide

1. **Create a New Cart**:
   - When a user starts adding items to the cart for the first time, create a new document in the `carts` collection with a new `cartId`, linking to the user with the `userId` field.

2. **Add Items to Cart**:
   - For each item added to the cart, create a new document in the `cartItems` subcollection within the user's `cart` document.
   - The `cartItems` document will reference the specific `productId`, and include the `quantity` and possibly the `price` at the time of addition.

3. **Update Cart Status**:
   - As items are added to or removed from the cart, and as the user proceeds to checkout, update the `status` field within the cart document accordingly.
   - The `lastUpdated` field helps track when the cart was last modified.

4. **Checkout Process**:
   - During checkout, you can directly reference the `cartItems` to compile the order summary.
   - After a successful payment, update the cart `status` to 'CheckedOut'.

5. **Security Rules**:
   - Implement security rules to ensure users can only interact with their carts and associated `cartItems`.

6. **Handling Abandoned Carts**:
   - You may use the `status` field to identify carts that haven’t been active for a certain period (e.g., 'Abandoned').

7. **Application Logic**:
   - Within your application, write logic to handle cart creation, adding/updating/removing items, and checkout workflows.
   - Ensure consistency by pulling product details directly from their respective `products` documents when needed, for example, to display the current price in the cart's UI.

This structure enables you to manage customer carts effectively and provides flexibility for customers to modify their carts before finalizing their orders. It also ensures that the cart data is directly tied to the user and provides a clear path from adding items to the cart all the way through to the checkout process.

### Update ShopProfiles Collection to Include Branches Subcollection
